title: Vestigial Nematic Order at Zero Temperature in Two-Dimensional Frustrated Quantum Antiferromagnets
abstract: The phase diagram of the two-dimensional quantum $J_1$-$J_3$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a square lattice is a long-standing open problem. Despite recent advances in numerical techniques for quantum spin models, a detailed analytical theory is still lacking. We address this problem using a semiclassical approach based on a continuum nonlinear sigma model effective field theory, applying the nonperturbative large-$N$ technique to map out the phase diagram and determine the magnetic correlations. We show that previously-overlooked interactions are crucial for stabilizing a vestigial nematic phase, both at finite and zero temperature. Our results reveal that the spontaneous breaking of global symmetries in the $J_1$-$J_3$ model is controlled by the strength of infrared quantum fluctuations which are enhanced by proximity to the classical Lifshitz point.
summary: This theoretical work investigates the complex phase diagram of a frustrated quantum antiferromagnet using advanced field theory techniques. It reveals that the system can stabilize a vestigial nematic order, even when long-range magnetic order is destroyed by quantum fluctuations. This provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding symmetry breaking in frustrated quantum magnets.
main_problem: The paper addresses the long-standing open problem of determining the quantum phase diagram of the two-dimensional $J_1$-$J_3$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet.
main_result: The study shows that previously overlooked interactions are crucial for stabilizing a vestigial nematic phase, which persists down to zero temperature, separating different quantum disordered and ordered regimes.
method: The analysis employs a semiclassical approach using a continuum nonlinear sigma model effective field theory, enhanced by the nonperturbative large-$N$ technique.
model_or_system: The system is the two-dimensional quantum $J_1$-$J_3$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a square lattice, described by a Hamiltonian involving nearest-neighbor ($J_1$) and next-nearest-neighbor ($J_3$) interactions.
key_observables: Nematic order parameters ($\Psi_1, \Psi_2$), static structure factor $S(q)$, and the correlation length $\xi$.
important_parameters: The ratio of coupling constants ($J_1, J_3$), the large-$N$ limit, and the proximity to the classical Lifshitz point.
main_assumptions: The method is semiclassical and formally exact only in the limit $S 	o \infty$; the analysis often relies on assuming certain order parameters are energetically suppressed ($\Psi_1 \equiv 0$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram showing the coexistence of spiral, vestigial nematic, and quantum disordered regions, and plot susceptibilities and structure factors across different parameter regimes.
paper_structure: The paper builds from introducing the continuum effective field theory, applying the large-$N$ technique to derive the effective potential, and finally solving the resulting saddle-point equations to map out the phase diagram.
why_interesting: The focus on symmetry breaking (nematicity) and quantum fluctuations near a critical point is highly relevant to understanding exotic quantum phases in strongly correlated materials like cuprates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The dimensionless inverse correlation length ξ−1√b1/T, where ξ−2 = λ0 for η0 > 0 and ξ−2 = λ0 −η2 0/4bQ for η0 < 0, and (b) the dimensionless physical stiffness η0/T, both as a function of the renormalized stiffness parameter ρR/T, for b2 = 0, µ2b1/T 2 = 1 and γ0/b1 = 2. In (b), the physical stiffness, which is modified by higher derivative inter- actions, is compared to the renormalized stiffness parameter shown by the dashed gray line, which is the stiffness of the theory in the absence of higher derivative interactions.

FIG. 2. Classical nematic susceptibility as a function of ρR/T for b2 = 0, µ2b1/T = 1, γ0/b1 = 2 and γ2/γ0 = −1/8. Here ρcΨ/T ≈−1.2. For ρR < ρcΨ the susceptibility in the nematic phase follows from the Ginzburg-Landau analysis as χΨ = |2Γ(0,2)[0]|−1.

FIG. 3. Fourth order Ginzburg-Landau coefficient (arbitrary units) as a function of ρR/T for b2 = 0, µ2b1/T = 1, γ0/b1 = 2 and γ2/γ0 = −1/8. Here ρcΨ/T ≈−1.2 and the tricritical point marked with the yellow dot is at ρR/T ≈−0.3.

FIG. 4. (a) The dimensionless mass gap m0/µ, where m2 0 = λ0 for η0 > 0 and m2 0 = λ0 −η2 0/4bQ for η0 < 0. The N´eel and spiral ordering transitions occur where the gap closes at ρcN and ρcS, respectively. (b) The dimensionless physical stiffness η0/µ√b1, both as a function of the dimensionless renormal- ized stiffness parameter ρR/µ√b1, for b2 = 0, gR/√b1 = 8π and γ0/b1 = 2. In (b), the dimensionless physical stiffness is compared to the dimensionless renormalized stiffness param- eter ρR/µ√b1 shown by the dashed gray line.

FIG. 5. Second order coefficient in the Ginzburg-Landau ex- pansion of the effective potential for b2 = 0, gR/√b1 = 8π, γ0/b1 = 2 and γ2/γ0 = −1/8. The inset shows a magnified view of the region in the dashed box. The dotted vertical line indicates the location of the pole in Γ(0,2)[0].
